context.scenario: AITA because i have this one classmate (f13) me (f15), i am her friend but she says she has autism and adhd, she usually yells in our classroom and doesnt listen to teachers, she skips classes and acts like a kid, ive been nice to her and helping her to her best to be calm, so ive asked her a lot times to calm down, as generally im a nice person. Lately shes been telling me to leave that job to the teachers but she wont listen to the teachers and the teachers go to me to calm her down. So i told her to stop talking babyish and acting like an animal and shes mad at me. AITA?
context.comment:
NTA, except to yourself - you're not this person's friend at this point, you are their special needs support worker, except not even being paid for it.

The fact that your friend is getting mad at you shows that she has some understanding of her behaviours not being acceptable - which is the beginning of learning to be able to manage them. Learning disabled does not, repeat NOT, mean learning UNable, and if a qualified adult - the teacher - cannot get her to not be disruptive, then why should you - legally still a child, and not qualified - be expected to take responsibility? 

The only way your friend will learn to seek support to manage her responses, or be able to obtain appropriate educational support if/as needed is if you STOP "fixing it".  Let her get on with it and take the consequences. Do not allow your own education and mental health to suffer.

context.scenario:
I (32F) have a lodger (31F) who’s been living in the spare room on my 2bed, 2bath flat. I “own” the flat under shared ownership (I pay mortgage on half and pay rent on the other). I had no intention of being a live in landlord, and don’t want to make any profit, so only charge what it costs for rent and 50% of the bills. 
I work full time 8:30 - 5:30 and my bedroom is next to the kitchen. I advertised for the room because my sister moved out (bought her own place nearer to her job), and it helps having company and not having to pay for an empty room.
In the initial ad, I shared that I essentially work a 9-5 and am looking for someone with a similar schedule because I’m sensitive to late night noise. All started fine, she was friendly enough, would go to work a few days a week, pay rent and bills on time and I hadn’t noticed anything strange, until…
This past month, my sleep pattern has been wrecked, I get woken up between 1am and 2am at least once or twice a week because she cooks late. It’s not just reheating food, it’s full on chopping, pots and pans, washing, fridge door slamming (to the point I can feel the wall shake), doors closing loudly, corridor lights on. I’ve bought earplugs, but I still feel like I have no say in when and how I can fall asleep. It seems she’s up until at least 2am and (?still) awake at 7am and sleeps all day. She seems to not be leaving the flat to go to work as often too.
I’ve asked her politely to keep it down, her “compromise” was to finish cooking at 1am, I stupidly agreed, but even with that, I’ve been woken up again tonight. This may be irrelevant, but it’s annoying, so I’ll say. She goes on about natural remedies, “energy boosting” supplements and complains about her poor mental health. As someone who’s recently had an admission for severe depression and has worked hard to sort out my sleep schedule, I feel all kinds of irritated by her.
I’m kind of stressed about confronting her, and sometimes worry I’m being uptight. WIBTA if I asked her plainly if she can adjust her schedule back to her “best behaviour” that she has at the start, otherwise I don’t think the living situation is sustainable.

TLDR; lodger is loud after 1am regularly, it’s running my mental health and I’m struggling to cope
context.comment:
I'm guessing you are in the UK from the use of 'lodger'

NTA: I think it's commonly accepted that 11 pm to 7 am are 'quiet hours' and your lodger **needs** to respect this - what if your neighbours also complain?

As she is an excluded occupier, I would just not get into this any further. Tell her that it's not working for you, and give her one month's notice in writing to move out, and return her deposit when she does (unless there are damages or you have to go to the expense of changing locks).

If she doesn't go at the end of that time period, wait until she goes out and change the locks. You have an absolute legal right to do this. You do still have a duty of care to her possessions: you do not have to give her any access to the property, you can arrange for a time for her to collect and hand them out to her. Have a friend or family member present for support!

She may call the police and claim that you are evicting her illegally. Sometimes individual police officers do not have correct knowledge of housing law: insist on speaking to a sergeant and inform them that your lodger does NOT have assured tenancy rights as you are a live in landlord: use the words 'excluded occupier under the Protection from Evictions Act 1977'.

context.scenario:
I (27F) work in a mid-sized office and have a coworker, Melissa (32F), who lives just a few blocks from me. A few months ago, she asked if she could start catching a ride with me in the mornings since her car was in the shop. I agreed because I figured it would be temporary and I wasn’t going out of my way. Well, her “temporary” ride turned into two months. I didn’t mind too much, but she’s often not ready when I get there. I’d text her when I’m outside, and then sit for 5–10 minutes waiting while she finishes her coffee, touches up her makeup, or one time bakes muffins to take to the office. Last week, I was already running late because of traffic, and I texted her saying I’d be there in 3 minutes and we really had to go. She replied “K!” but when I arrived, she made me wait another 7 minutes. We ended up getting to work late, and I got written up for being 15 minutes past start time. It was my first ever write-up and I was really upset.

After that, I told Melissa (calmly, I swear) that I couldn’t give her rides anymore unless she was actually ready when I got there. She kind of rolled her eyes and said “Okay, okay, don’t be dramatic.”

So yesterday, I didn’t stop at her place I just went straight to work. She texted me furiously and said she waited outside for 20 minutes and had to Uber. She showed up late and was clearly pissed, saying I “blindsided” her and “could’ve at least given her a heads-up.” I told her I was done being late because she couldn’t be on time.

Now she’s telling people in the office I “abandoned” her, and a couple coworkers have said I was “cold” about it and that it wouldn’t have killed me to be a little flexible.

AITA for just… cutting off the rides without another warning?
context.comment:
NTA - on the few occasions I've caught a lift to work because on another site, or whatever, you can bet that I am actually out on the street at the agreed time waiting (unless it's really raining, in which case I'll be in the undercover bit at the side of the house about 10 steps further back, in full view of the street)

Melissa is rude and entitled - and: how have your coworkers found out? That's right, because she's bitched about it to them. Which means you owe her no 'duty' of - well, anything - and I'd tell 'em straight that I'd already had one write up because she made you late, and sure, it wouldn't kill you to be flexible, except it could lose you your job. End of conversation, don't engage further - and if anyone carries on, HR or your manager.

Perhaps Melissa's car will magically emerge from the shop where it's been for months now.

context.scenario:
I had our first baby 3.5 months ago. He's adorable. I had a rough pregnancy with gestational diabetes, and honestly, my husband wasn't great to me while pregnant. Generally unsupportive. A lot of behaviour that our couples therapist called "horrible" and "contemptable". 

I dropped several specific hints about gifts I'd like for Mother's Day, and reminded him several times that Mother's Day was coming up. Wake up, nothing. Reminded him it's Mother's Day.   
  
We got home from our camping trip with his brother and his friend, and as I'm finishing unpacking things, I'm getting undressed to change but my boobs are leaking a TON of milk everywhere. The baby is still asleep in his car seat, so I go ask my husband if he can just keep eyes on the baby while I go shower quick. Not have to do much, just keep his eyes on the baby. He rolls his eyes at me. Argues that he's on the phone with his mom and it's really important to him to talk to her on Mother's Day. I angrily say "I need you to watch the baby for just a few minutes on Mother's Day for the person you recently willingly made a mother." and just go in to shower.

I'm now steamed in more ways than one, and he goes to take the dog to the park. On the way home from the park, he picked up a greeting card and books at a bookstore that was still open. I do appreciate the effort, although comically one of the two books is a children's book about how great dads are in the animal kingdom. In all fairness, I just don't think he read the book. I do like seahorses. 

Of course, we argued about it for days. The merits of my irritation. That it wasn't fair of me to ask him to watch the baby and interrupt his precious call with his mother. 

Now to the recent kicker, last night we talked in circles for what seemed like an hour about how he would have liked to see his mom on Mother's Day. I had talked to her before Mother's Day, and she very specifically said she and her husband have plans. I even tried to look up restaurants that are halfway, but she turned me down. Now, apparently, my husband says that when he later reiterated that he wanted to see her, my repeating that she turned me down to hang out was her way of politely letting us have a day together. (Some day that was) I was supposed to read between the lines. And now he's mad at me for not showing him empathy in that situation? That he'd liked to have seen his mom? How does that work?
context.comment:
NTA, sounds like you have two babies, not one.

As far as your husband goes - why didn't HE talk to his mother before Mothers' Day about arrangements?  
He's a grown man, why is it up to YOU to "make sure" he sees his mother on Mothers' Day?

Maybe MIL was 'being polite' and letting the two of you have your first Mothers' Day together: how are you supposed to know that? Even if it's true, that was her choice/decision.

Honestly though, sounds like your therapist is not very professional actually saying your husband's behaviour is 'horrible' and 'contemptible' - even if it is. It's couples therapy, IMO the therapist should be challenging him with questions why he is behaving how he does and getting HIM to recognise that it's horrible etc, rather than the therapist labelling it, which will just lead to husband shutting down or seeing the therapist as taking sides and ignoring anything else they say.

context.scenario: I (M17) got a job at this diner type place about a few weeks ago, and its not a bad job at all, but i have an unfortunate last name (smallwood), and these people wont stop talking about it. I get it if we were friends, but i barely know these people. Along with this one of my coworkers (25ish) keeps asking me sexual ass questions like what coworkers id fuck and all that, im not a horny ass dude so i just ignored him, honestly 1 or 2 is whatever but its constant and its starting to weird me out. after this treatment, i started not being enthusiastic to talk to these people, which they said is disrespectful and told my boss who said he will fire me if im being disrespectful. I think its weird that they would say all this shit then play victim, should i of kept just taking this? Its annoying, and they don’t stop. Obviously i don’t want to throw hands at work but im not sure what to do.
context.comment:
The continuous sexual jokes in relation to your name are not not funny: they are bullying you. 

Not responding is the mature response, definitely don't throw hands as that would put you in the wrong. What you should be doing is dealing with it - you really need to tell your boss: if they believe that you are being disrespectful by not joining in jokes at your expense then you need a new job with people who can behave like adults. 

There is a difference between "snitching" and 'raising a valid concern.' and these behaviours cross that line. You are young and learning how to navigate shit like this, so NTA.

You could verbally bat back with "Why are you so obsessed with my sex life? It's weird and makes me not want to talk to you." or if the joke relates to your name and is one of the frequent repeat ones, then it's OK to roll your eyes and slightly sarcastically say "Like I've never heard THAT one before. It's not like I can help my name, please stop going on about it." or in a pitying tone, ask them if their life is so boring that they have nothing to talk about other than your HILARIOUS name. Bear in mind that either of the last two ARE disrespectful and you are matching energy, sometimes it actually makes people stop and think though - I have a name that is also "hilarious" to some people, I'll let it go a couple of times, after that, the same joke from the same people gets one of my 'knock it off' responses.

context.scenario:
Okay so my (25F) girlfriend (25F) and I were cleaning up my apartment tonight. For context we are both vet students and both busy with classes and exams coming up. I kept telling her how much I appreciate her helping me as I was feeling pretty overwhelmed. 

As I was finishing sweeping she asked if she could rearrange the litter boxes. I said no not right now maybe we can take a look at that later. I guess I could’ve been more open but I was hyperfixated on the task I was doing. I turn around and she’s rearranging the litter boxes anyway, I made a slightly annoyed sigh but let it go. When I came back in the room she wasn’t talking to me and I asked her what was wrong? She started going off that I was so rude and what was so bad about her idea, I just said that I was sorry and if it made her happy that way than that’s fine. She just kept going. 

A little while later I sat down to try and explain where I was coming from, I told her that sometimes when I’m overstimulated and hyper focused on a different task that I can be a bit sharp which wasn’t fair to her and that I was sorry. I told her I just wished that when I told her no to doing that right now that she’d just respect that and have a conversation with me later on (we’ve had a similar conversation about a cat tree). She said that that makes her feel like she’s gentle parenting a two year old and didn’t see the big deal. This obviously felt really hurtful as I was only trying to find a healthy way for us to communicate. She just started blankly at me in a condescending way and I left the conversation feeling worse than I did before. So  AITA?
context.comment:
It's YOUR apartment, or shared? - does she actually live there? Does she pay costs towards it if she does?

If she doesn't live there, she respects the 'No' in relation to any rearrangements / non urgent 'chores', no ifs, no buts. 

Even if you share the space - well, if my housemate started randomly moving shit around shared spaces in my house without even saying anything, let alone asking if OK, I would be super annoyed and I don't have any neurodivergency diagnosis: it is normal for someone to expect their space to be respected. 

Rearranging the litter boxes does not sound like a time sensitive task (like, say, cleaning them so they don't smell/pets need clean toileting facilities). So even if your girlfriend is living with you and paying half, she needs to respect the "later" and ask later.

NTA

context.scenario:
Alright, am I (F) the asshole for telling my roommate (F) that her boyfriend can’t come over because i’m scared of him? 

My roommates boyfriend is in my eyes abusive, he yells at her, calls her names, throws stuff around her room, the other day he tried to steal her car, he also goes on 4 day benders with dr*gs, constantly calls and texts her saying that it’s all her fault that he’s like this, he has also smashed up her car. 

He reminds me of a very toxic and abusive ex that i’ve had before she is aware of this fact, i know she’s in love with him and she wants to be around him, however whenever he’s over i’m terrified and end up not being able to sleep cause im concerned he’s going to hurt her and ill wake to her seriously injured or even dead. 

I’m trying to maybe find a middle ground saying i’m okay if he comes over on the weekends to stay the night and just come and hangout during the week then go home, but she says that it’s like i’m trying to parent her and she feels like shes suffocated, obviously i feel horrible because im stuck in between to places my comfort and her comfort.

I’m not sure what to do because we are both adults, i just really don’t like him, she’s been starting to just stay out super late at night with him and just not come home until late, and im just super uncomfortable with the idea that keeps coming to mind that she’s sneaking him in (she isn’t i just feel like she is), she is also saying it super unfair and i understand why she feels like that because i still have my boyfriend over but only on the weekends cause we both work i feel like a horrible roommate however i ask if he can come over and he is super respectful of her and we don’t scream or fight or make anything uncomfortable for the house. 

I really don’t like this guy and i have to stress that she is trying to respect my feelings but she’s getting more and more angry because of the olive branch of him coming over on the weekends and feeling restricted and i’m scared that i’m making things horrible 

please give me some advice, thank you
context.comment:
NTA

Although I think you need to be really clear that it's not him coming over that's the problem, it's his \*behaviour\* - you have touched on this lower down in your post and I think you might need to be explicit that if her boyfriend behaves threateningly by yelling or throwing stuff around - even if you are not the target - you WILL call the police as those behaviours make you feel unsafe. 

You are not responsible for your room-mate. Hard though it is to stand by while someone is in what sounds like a toxic relationship, that is what you have to do. Get out of the lease as soon as you can as 'things' are going to be horrible either way. At least insist that they are peaceful if they're going to be horrible.

context.scenario: i'm so sorry if this isn't coherent, i'm sitting in a parking lot crying. so i (18F) have been dating my bf (21M) for a little over 8 months and he used to be a hard core alcoholic until we started dating when he ended up barely drinking for months. recently he's started drinking again and every time it causes fights in our relationship which he blames on me. i have been going through a really difficult time in my life because my grandma(who is my best friend), was recently diagnosed with untreatable cancer has just been told she has weeks left (months if she's lucky). my boyfriend's friends all love drinking and they only seem to have a good time if alcohol is involved, which i hear about in the stories he tells me from the nights they go drinking. today might finally have been my last straw. we had plans to go hangout with one of his two not alcoholic friends who actually like me and are nice to me but then one of his other friends ended up tagging along last minute so he left me to go hangout with them, and promised me he wouldn't drink. not even an hour later, i get a call from him saying he is going to drink and that kinda just set me off. i asked why he always switches up on me especially when alcohol is involved and he tried to flip it around to being a me problem, which he tries doing in every argument. i told him that im tired of him lying about changing and apologized for not being a good enough girlfriend for him to stick to keeping his promise of changing. he asked me if i can pick him up tn, at 12. now im just crying in this random parking lot until then cause im to embarrassed to go home and tell my mom. i feel like i should leave him but i just want to know im good enough for him to change. i dont really know what to do with myself anymore. i hate starting over but i dont know if i can stay with him any longer. also he's getting me a kitten which we aren't picking up until sometime in july and i might just break up with him after i get it.
context.comment:
NTA. This is classic addict behaviour.

He is an adult, who makes his own choices. He CHOOSES to drink. It's not your fault. He will NOT CHANGE until HE decides to change, and you will NEVER be "good enough" - no-one will be, or should ever have to be.

The ONLY reason to try to stay with it would be if you were financially forced to (shared ownership of a house) or had children together. Even then, your own wellbeing is paramount.

You have neither of those reasons. Leave. Seriously. Now. Run, don't walk. Block his number. He. Will. Not. Change. 

Also, kitten, WTAF? Just break up with him now for goodness sake, and get some therapy or at least do some reading to understand addiction and co-dependency, because that is what is happening here.

context.scenario:
I (17 F) and my sister (18 F) have been very close growing up so “snitching” was not a normal thing for us. My sister came home one day from school and said to me “come look at this” she opened her mouth and I see a long metal tongue peircing and I  gag not expecting what she showed me, I ask her what it is, so she tells me it’s a tongue peircing and that I couldn’t tell anyone. And in my obvious fashion I said “nah I won’t”

And it’s been 2 weeks so far and she has been non stop complaining about how much it hurts all the time. My parents have non stop interrogating me knowing I can’t keep secrets very well. And it hasn’t been about the piercing in particular but also saying things like “you seem off you keeping a secret or something?” Scaring the shit out of me every-time.

Since she is 18 all my friends are telling me that my parents cannot get frustrated with her since she is a legal adult and she can do what she wants. 
I get that but also she is still living at home so I feel like my parents also would have a say on these kind of things. 
Thanks for reading! I appreciate any feedback
context.comment:
NTA

Do be aware that there needs to be some boundaries around this - if she is doing something life threatening (to herself, or others, or both, like drinking and driving or similar) then it's not "snitching" - it's raising a valid concern.

I think you need to reassure your parents that you would tell them if something was dangerous: otherwise, your sister's secrets are not yours to tell, and they need to speak to her directly. You may also need to be straight with your sister that your parents ARE putting you under pressure, so you'd actually prefer to not know about her 'stuff', then there isn't anything for you to hide.

context.scenario:
I (23F), am considering ending things with someone I've been seeing (25m)because I (and my friends) suspect he might be involved in a recent robbery that happened at my place. Here's the backstory;

My apartment was robbed last month, and my phone, laptop, and some cash were stolen. The guy I'm seeing was the last person at my apartment that night, although he didn't enter. After the robbery, he went MIA for 4 days without any support or contact.

The other thing that's bothering me is the fact that he's been to my place countless time when I on the other hand don't even know where he stays.

My friends think he might be involved in the robbery due to his suspicious behavior and lack of support afterward. I'm torn because I don't want to accuse him without proof, but I also don't feel comfortable continuing the relationship with these doubts.

I'm planning to cut him off without confronting him directly about the robbery. WIBTA for doing so?
context.comment:
NTA

You have no evidence that it was him, only suspicions. 

Even assuming you ever got solid proof it wasn't him: not knowing where he lives after he has been to your place 'countless times' is a walking red flag anyway - if he isn't willing to share this then you aren't important to him. He could be living with/married to someone else for a start. Not bothering to offer some emotional support after a traumatic event like a home invasion and robbery - second red flag.

At dating stage you don't owe anyone any detailed explanation other than something along the lines of a polite "I don't think this is going to work out between us, thanks for some good times".

context.scenario:
I broke up with my girlfriend 1 month ago. We had been living together for 2.5 years, and we have a cat that we always had together. He was always like a child to us. 

However, now that we have broken up, she moved out of the house and went back to her parents house. As part of the breakup process, I feel a strong need to move out of my current place, as this only brings back memories of being with her and it's just very bad for my mental health. The problem is that it's almost impossible for me to find a place to rent that allows pets. Additionally, I've just gotten a new job, and even though the majority of the time I work remotely, it does require me to travel multiple times per month, which would mean that the cat would be alone lots of times..

It's difficult for me because I do love this cat with all my heart, but I feel like I'm not in a position to take good care of him anymore. My ex-gf says she can't take it because her parents already have a cat, and says that if she brings another one, they won't get along and will fight all the time. 

The only alternative I see is just trying to find a new owner for him.. or otherwise I'd have to lie to the new landlord (when I rent a new place) about not having any pets. But then again, it'll be difficult whenever I have to travel.

When I briefly mentioned this to my ex-gf , she says I'm an asshole for even thinking about this.. and I'm now unsure if I'm actually being unreasonable?
context.comment:
NTA

Having to rehome your cat that you love is heartbreaking and it's **because** you love him that you are wanting to find him a better home. 

Please do make sure you check out prospective new owners, and he will settle fine with new people within a few days, you don't need to worry about him if he has gone to a nice home. Hope you can recover and one day find a place that will allow you to have another cat friend - not a replacement, a different one to love.

context.scenario:
I took an astronomy class in college called Search for Extraterrestrial Life. It was a really cool course where we learned about how astronomers search for Extraterrestrial life and how they search for possibly habitable planets. For our final project we split into groups and had to do research on one thing we've learned in class and present it through some type of media, she gave some examples like doing a newspaper or newscast. A lot of groups chose to make fake newspapers or magazines or small movies.

The group I joined met up to decide on what our project would be and literally all of my ideas were shut down. I had an idea to make a TikTok account where all the videos were about our topic, but everyone else in the group wanted to do a newspaper article because none of them used TikTok. I didn't want to do the newspaper article because a bunch of other groups were doing this. I also just didn't think the topic the other people in my group chose was that interesting. The project they chose made me feel very anxious because I didn't think it would do well.

Before my next class I went to the professor and asked if I could do the project on my own. I'd had other accommodations through the disability office that semester and she said that if I was anxious about the group then I could work on my own as an accommodation. I informed the group that I would be working on my own. This was super early in the project and we had not yet moved past the point of just coming up with the idea and they still had 4 members in the group. They all were pretty upset at me afterwards, which I understand. 

I did the project on my own and and j ended up making an A+. My rubric showed that I got the max amount of points I could for creativity and how interesting my project was. After the grades and rubrics went out, the guy who had made most of the suggestions for the group project told me that the group had only received a C and told me that I was an asshole. He showed me the rubric for the group and they barely got any points for crwativity because they did something a lot of groups did. He said that if I'd stayed they would have done better, but I thought I might be just ended up with a C like they did. 

AITA for deciding to do my own project since I didn't like the groups concensus?
context.comment:
NTA, although as others have said, you will need to learn to compromise when working with others once you get into the work environment, which is part of the skills that this exercise was trying to teach you.

Please also recognise that you cannot and must not enforce any kind of social media membership on anyone.

context.scenario:
My cousin loves going out to bars and clubs. she invited me to go out with her last weekend, but i told her i wasn’t interested because i don’t like drinking and didn’t want to get drunk. I went out with her once to a club but I just felt bored and wouldn't wanna clubbing again

she kept pushing, saying i didn’t have to drink and could just come for fun. but then she made a comment that made me realize what she really wanted—she said, "come on, i don’t wanna be out alone. who’s gonna have my back if something happens?"

that’s when it hit me. she wasn’t inviting me because she wanted to hang out—she wanted me there to watch over her while she got wasted. i told her if she wasn’t comfortable going out alone, maybe she shouldn’t be going at all. she got mad and accused me of being a bad cousin for not helping her out.
My other older cousin said i should have just gone with her since "that’s what family does," but i don’t think it’s fair to be guilt-tripped into being her babysitter..

aita for refusing to go out drinking with her?
context.comment:
NTA

No-one, whoever they are and whoever is with them should EVER be getting so wasted in public that they can't get themselves safely home.

Hold your ground. If she goes anyway and 'something happens' then that is NOT on you: she is the one choosing to drink to the point where she is no longer situationally aware.

context.scenario:
I (29F) have a nephew, "Zayn" (5Y), who is a great kid but can be really unruly. His parents—my brother and sister-in-law—don’t seem to discipline him much.

Recently, they came over to my house for dinner, and Zayn was running around, jumping on my couch with his shoes on, messing with things on my shelves, and even knocking over a drink. His parents never tell him to stop.

After he nearly broke a decorative piece, I told him, *"Zayn, please stop running and be careful with things in my house."* He got quiet and sat down, but my sister-in-law got offended. 

I feel like I had every right to set rules in my own home, but now she is acting like I overstepped. AITA for telling my nephew to behave when his parents wouldn’t?
context.comment:
NTA

You were polite and made a reasonable request. Assuming no learning disability, a 5-year old is not too young to understand some basic manners when visiting someone else's house - like not jumping on furniture and asking before handling items.

Alternatively, move all breakables before visits, and it might be nice to get some lego or something in to keep Zayn occupied? - which stays at "auntie's house" as something to look forward to visits to you.

scenario:
I have lived with my roommate for over 8months now, her lowkey helicopter mother told me beforehand that because of her illness(bladder/ digestive problems) she let her be a little spoiled and would just cleaned up after her and asked for me to be patient with her lack of cleanliness and to wake her up everyday? now that i think abt it idk why i agreed but i was like "kind of a weird thing to ask me but sure i guess", but i didn't think it was gonna be an issue until i actually saw it. 

She wouldn't do the dishes, clean up after herself, flush the toilet, or basically any shared housework unless i specifically reminded her (and it gets a little tiring after months of that), she also had a weird habit of throwing food down the sink and ot starts smelling real bad if i wait for her to clean it up. She would also have terrible table manners and would leave food directly on the couch and stain it then leave it as it is, which was pretty darn difficult to get off since the couch was made of fabric. But whenever i point it out, at first she says she'll stop but the next day i find her doing the same thing.

Now my own parents were aware of all that and at first they were also pissed, but lately they've been telling me that my roommate was just very ill and i should be patient but honestly i don't care i don't think being ill justifies her making a mess around and me having to be patient, waking her up everyday and still cleaning up after her? 

Btw, for context we're both uni students but she barely attends any classes so im ngl after coming back from my classes to a mess and weird odors, its not the best feeling out there but i might be overreacting.

AITA?
question: Based on this person's historical commenting patterns, which moral principle would they MOST likely use to judge this scenario?
answer_options: A. Autonomy/Boundaries: The judgment is primarily justified by violation of personal sovereignty: controlling behavior, unwanted interference, privacy invasion, or boundary crossing (non-material).
B. Role-based Responsibility: The judgment is primarily justified by duties tied to a specific functional role (tenant/roommate/employee/co-parent) rather than emotion-based loyalty.
C. Care/Harm Prevention: The judgment is primarily justified by concern about direct physical or emotional suffering of a person (victim-centered empathy, trauma, distress).
D. Property/Consent: The judgment is primarily justified by violations involving ownership, money, physical possessions, or bodily access without permission.
